title: Risotto alle cozze
subtitle: Mussel risotto with white wine and parsley
excerpt: Mussels steamed open in white wine, their dark, briny liquor strained and used as the stock for a risotto. No cheese: Italian convention holds that grated Parmesan has no place alongside seafood, and the mussel cooking liquid provides all the depth and seasoning the rice needs. Finished with olive oil and parsley.
sourceNotes: Risotto alle cozze is a southern Italian and Adriatic coastal preparation, with regional variants documented from Puglia northward through the Veneto. The convention against serving grated cheese with seafood pasta and risotto is widely noted in Italian regional cooking literature; Artusi addresses the cheese-and-seafood distinction in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain). The technique of using mussel cooking liquor as the risotto stock is documented throughout Adriatic coastal cookbooks.

content.text: The mussel liquor is the point of this dish. When mussels steam open in white wine, they release a liquid that is deeply saline, slightly sweet, and intense in a way that no shop-bought stock can match. Strain it carefully, leaving behind any grit at the bottom, and use it to cook the risotto. Every ladle carries the sea into the rice.

attrs.body: Italian culinary convention is firm on this: grated Parmesan does not belong in a seafood risotto. The convention exists because aged cheese competes with and masks the more delicate flavour of fish and shellfish. The mussel liquor provides the seasoning. Finish with extra-virgin olive oil instead of butter.
attrs.title: No cheese with seafood

items.name: Mussels
items.prepNote: scrubbed and debearded; discard any that stay open when tapped

items.name: Dry white wine
items.prepNote: 100 ml for steaming mussels, 100 ml for risotto

items.name: Garlic
items.prepNote: 2 left whole for mussels, 2 finely sliced for risotto

items.name: Fine sea salt
items.prepNote: taste before adding; mussel liquor is already salty

content.text: Put the {{mussels}} in a large saucepan with 100 ml of the {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine and 2 whole garlic cloves. Cover and cook over high heat for 3 to 4 minutes, shaking the pan once, until the mussels have opened. Discard any that remain closed. Lift the mussels out with a slotted spoon. Remove most of the meat from the shells, leaving a few in shell for presentation. Pour the cooking liquid through a fine sieve lined with kitchen paper, leaving any grit behind. Add enough water to bring it to 1 litre. Season very lightly and keep hot.

Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide, heavy pan over medium heat. Add the {{shallot}} shallots and sliced {{garlic}} of garlic. Cook for 5 minutes until soft and translucent. Add the chilli flakes if using. Add the {{arborio-rice}} of risotto rice and stir for 2 minutes until the grains look translucent at the edges.

Pour in the remaining 100 ml of white wine. Stir until absorbed. Add the hot mussel stock a ladleful at a time, stirring constantly and waiting for each addition to absorb before adding the next. Cook for 16 to 18 minutes until the rice is creamy and just cooked through with a slight bite.

Stir in the mussel meat and half the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley. Cook for 1 minute to warm through. Remove from heat. Stir in the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of extra-virgin olive oil. Taste for salt, the mussel liquor is briny, so add very little if any. Serve immediately, garnished with the remaining parsley and any mussels left in shell.

items.fix: Season the mussel stock only lightly; taste the risotto before adding any extra salt
items.cause: Mussel liquor fully seasoned before adding; the stock concentrates as it is ladled in

items.fix: Add the mussel meat only in the last minute of cooking; they need only to warm through, not cook again
items.cause: Added to the risotto too early and continued cooking for too long

items.fix: Pour slowly through a fine sieve lined with kitchen paper; leave the last centimetre of liquid in the pan
items.cause: Strained too quickly, carrying the sediment from the bottom of the pan

items.fix: Use the freshest mussels available; reduce the amount of water used to top up if the stock seems diluted
items.cause: Mussels were not very fresh, or too much water used to top up the stock
items.symptom: Risotto tastes flat despite using mussel stock

content.text: Risotto alle cozze is at home along the entire Adriatic coast, from Trieste south to Puglia, wherever the sea is close and mussels are farmed or gathered. The dish belongs to the cucina povera tradition of coastal Italy. Shellfish were once cheap and plentiful, and rice was a storable staple. The pairing of mussel liquor and starchy rice is a piece of practical cooking that happens to be excellent. The rule against cheese with seafood feels arbitrary until you taste both versions side by side. The restraint turns out to be correct.
